failure_modes.text: Teams can invoke Black Swan language to justify vague fear, permanent defensiveness, or refusal to prioritize even when concrete resilience moves are available.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use simulations and stress-testing to convert vague tail-risk fear into concrete exposure boundaries, buffers, and monitoring thresholds.

failure_modes.text: An aberration can be over-weighted until strategy starts treating the outlier as the new normal.
failure_modes.mitigation: Contextualize the aberration: step back, zoom out, and compare against reference-class data to confirm whether the outlier represents a structural shift or a statistical extreme.

failure_modes.text: Hindsight can rewrite history and make a genuinely surprising event feel obvious after the fact, blocking real learning.
failure_modes.mitigation: Enforce post-mortem discipline: resist rewriting history, focus on calibrating beliefs, and invite team members to share what they were genuinely uncertain about before the event.

premortem_questions.text: If this decision fails, what is the least obvious reason why that we are not discussing now?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this decision leads to failure, what is the least obvious reason why, that we are not discussing now?

premortem_questions.text: What core strategy must we execute regardless of whether this specific catastrophic event occurs or not?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What core strategy must we execute, regardless of whether this specific catastrophic event occurs or not?

premortem_questions.text: If one essential component fails unexpectedly, what cascading implications should we already be prepared for?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If one essential component of our complex system fails unexpectedly, what are the cascading implications?

heuristics.text: Use systems thinking to inspect interrelationships and failure propagation rather than isolated events.
Shift from prediction toward scenario analysis and outcome simulation when deep uncertainty makes point forecasts brittle.
Separate good decisions from good outcomes so bad luck does not automatically discredit a sound process.
heuristics.source_quote: a good decision may sometimes lead to a bad outcome

curation_notes.summary: The source is naturally chunk-ready on unknown unknowns, fragility in complex systems, tail-risk theater, and anti-hindsight discipline.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broad uncertainty rhetoric because the raw source is stronger on resilience, simulation, and fragility checks.
open_questions: The third heuristic compresses the decision-quality versus outcome-quality distinction so the chunk stays operational.

allies: The raw ally surface is strongest on systems-thinking, resilience, and checklist-like execution discipline because the model is about readiness under surprise.
antagonists: The raw antagonist pressure is strongest on hindsight-bias and anchoring because both distort learning and underestimate complex-system fragility.
structured_tensions: The source explicitly names hindsight-bias and anchoring as conflicts, which makes conservative current-vocabulary relation preservation possible.
